content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

Final part of a 3-part series. Part 1 built self-describing pages; Part 2 controlled what Google crawls. This part covers the inverse discipline deliberate exclusion plus rendering and the synthesis.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Across Part 1 and Part 2, we built pages that describe themselves and a crawl strategy that points Google only at real, canonical URLs. The last layers are about restraint: keeping the wrong pages out of the index -without ever accidentally dropping a good one.
Layer 6: Knowing when not to be indexed
Great programmatic SEO is as much about what you exclude as what you include. Two edge cases get explicit noindex treatment:
Expired jobs. When a job is gone (HTTP 410), our server still returns a 200 shell (so a logged-in user’s browser can refetch with auth). But a 200 with dead content is a soft-404 in Google’s eyes — so generateMetadata explicitly returns { index: false, follow: false } for it.
Confirmed-empty filter pages. If a valid filter combo genuinely matches zero jobs, it gets { index: false, follow: true }.
⚠️ The distinction that saves your rankings. On a fetch failure, the count stays null, and the page is not noindexed. A backend outage must never cause valid, ranking pages to be silently de-indexed. noindex fires only when the count is definitively zero, never on an unknown.
Layer 7 & 8: Rendering and installability
Two supporting layers round it out.
SSR + ISR for indexability. Listing and detail pages are async server components. The HTML a crawler receives is fully rendered — JSON-LD, headings, and meta all present in the initial payload, not hydrated client-side. Interactive bits live in isolated ...Client components below the server shell. Incremental Static Regeneration (revalidate of 300s on detail pages, 3600s on sitemaps) keeps everything fresh without full rebuilds.
PWA / manifest. A generated manifest.ts (standalone display, maskable icons) makes the site installable, and a service worker handles offline caching in production. Beyond the install prompt, the payoff is Core Web Vitals faster repeat visits feed directly into ranking.
